title: Home milking a dairy goat
subtitle: Milking stand setup, udder preparation, hand-milking technique, stripping, teat dipping, and safe milk storage.
excerpt: Milking a goat by hand is a learnable skill that takes most beginners a week or two to get consistent. The technique matters less than the routine: the same sequence every milking, carried out calmly, produces more milk and fewer mastitis problems than an erratic approach. This tutorial covers everything from the milking stand to storing the milk safely.
sourceNotes:
British Goat Society, Milking and Milk Hygiene Guidance. https://www.allgoats.com
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Goats (1991). https://www.gov.uk
NADAIS Goat Milk Production Notes. https://www.nadis.org.uk

glossaryTerms.definition: The release of milk from the alveoli (milk-producing cells) into the cistern and teat canal, triggered by the hormone oxytocin. Let-down is stimulated by udder washing, the sound of the milk pail, or the presence of the kid. It lasts 5 to 8 minutes. Milking must begin promptly after stimulation and be completed before the let-down response fades; delayed or incomplete milking reduces yield and milk-out.

The final action of milking, in which the remaining milk is drawn from the teat canal using a gentle downward squeeze after the main milk-out is complete. Stripping removes the fat-rich hindmilk and empties the teat canal, which reduces the risk of mastitis. It typically yields a small additional quantity of milk after the main flow has slowed to drops.

The application of a barrier or disinfectant solution to the teat end immediately after milking to prevent bacterial entry through the teat canal while the sphincter is still open. Post-milking teat dips typically contain iodine or glycerine at concentrations specified by the product label. Pre-milking teat dips are also used in higher-yield setups but are optional in small home herds.

content.text: A dairy goat in good milk will produce 1 to 3 litres per day depending on breed, stage of lactation, and feeding. She needs milking twice a day at roughly 12-hour intervals for a full lactation, or once a day in the latter part of the lactation when yield drops. Missing or skipping milkings puts pressure on the udder and risks mastitis. The routine you establish in the first week tends to stick.

A milking stand lifts the goat to a comfortable working height and secures her head in a stanchion while she eats a small ration of concentrate. This keeps her still, makes udder access straightforward, and turns milking into a positive experience the goat will willingly walk onto. A stand can be built from treated timber or bought ready-made. The platform should be at least 50 cm off the ground and non-slip. Fit a bucket hook below the teat end so the pail does not tip.

Wash each teat and the lower udder with warm water on a clean cloth or individual teat wipe. Dry thoroughly with a separate paper towel or cloth. This removes surface bacteria, warms the udder, and stimulates

. Strip the first two squirts from each teat into a strip cup or onto the floor. The first milk from the teat canal is the highest-bacteria fraction; discarding it reduces contamination in the pail. It also lets you check for lumpy, watery, or discoloured milk, which indicates mastitis.

Wrap your thumb and forefinger around the teat at its base, close to the udder. Squeeze gently to trap milk in the teat. Then close the remaining fingers in sequence from forefinger to little finger, forcing the milk out of the teat end. Release the pressure and let the teat refill from the cistern above. Repeat rhythmically. Do not pull downward on the teat: the teat is not a handle. Pulling causes discomfort and can damage the suspensory ligament of the udder over time. Aim for a rate of 60 to 80 squeezes per minute per hand, alternating teats.

attrs.title: The first week
attrs.body: New milkers often find their forearms ache after the first few sessions. The grip strength builds quickly. If the goat is fidgeting or kicking, check that the teat preparation was thorough, that the stand is stable, and that you are not pulling downward. A restless goat is usually telling you something is uncomfortable.

content.text: When the main flow slows to drops,

removes the fat-rich hindmilk and empties the teat canal. Use a gentle sliding squeeze from the base to the teat end, drawing out the remaining milk. Strip each teat three or four times. The hindmilk is thicker than the fore milk and adds to both the yield and the fat content of what you have collected.

to each teat immediately. Dip cups are small cups with a soft collar; fill them with your chosen product and dip each teat so the liquid covers the teat end. The teat canal sphincter takes 15 to 30 minutes to close after milking; during that window bacteria can enter. Post-dip dramatically cuts mastitis rates even on small herds. Keep the goat standing for a few minutes after milking, rather than lying down immediately, to give the sphincter time to begin closing.

Strain the milk through a disposable milk filter into a clean, lidded container. Cool it to below 4 degrees Celsius within one hour of milking. A domestic fridge works if the milk goes in while still warm but the fridge is not too full. Milk that is cooled slowly or stored above 4 degrees develops off flavours quickly. Use or refrigerate within 24 hours for drinking; goat milk for cheese can be held for up to 48 hours at below 4 degrees. Clean all milking equipment immediately after every milking with hot water and a dairy-approved cleaner.
